lessons.title: C++26 context
lessons.motivation: Before diving into specific C++26 features, it's worth understanding two things: what actually changed in the language in this revision of the standard, and how the course itself is structured. Without the first, it's hard to prioritize — for example, to understand why reflection and contracts each get a whole module, while small features like `_` get a single lesson. Without the second, it's easy to confuse "Skipped" with "Done", or to miss that a module's progress is measured by its mastery check, not by simply opening its lessons.
lessons.theory:
C++ is an international ISO/IEC standard that defines the language and the standard library. The standard evolves iteratively: the WG21 working group adopts proposals ("papers", in the form `PxxxxRN`, where `RN` is the proposal's revision number), which go through discussion, voting, and land in the next revision of the language. After C++23, the next revision on schedule is **C++26**.

**C++26 is finalized**: ISO ratified the standard on 28.03.2026, with the final text published as document **N5046**. This means every feature discussed in this course is not a draft or a "maybe it'll make it in" — it's a locked-in part of the language.

The first flagship is **Static Reflection** (based on proposal P2996). Before C++26, the language gave code no way to "look at itself": to find out at compile time which fields a struct has, what parameters a function takes, or what a type is named. Reflection introduces the `^^` operator (take the "reflection" of an entity) and `[: :]` (turn a reflection back into code), plus the `<meta>` library for working with these reflections. This opens the door to code generation without macros or external code generators — serialization, ORM-like wrappers, and automated tests can all be written in C++ itself. Module 5 covers this in depth.

The second flagship is **Contracts**. Previously, pre- and postcondition checks were written by hand using `assert` or `if`+`throw`, and were part of the implementation rather than the interface. C++26 adds the syntax `pre(...)`, `post(...)`, and `contract_assert(...)` to the language — contracts become a visible part of a function's declaration, with a configurable checking policy (enable, disable, or terminate). Module 4 covers this in detail.

The third flagship is **`std::execution`** (Senders/Receivers, P2300). It's a unified model for describing asynchronous and parallel computation in the standard library: instead of disparate APIs for threads, futures, and async I/O, there's a common "sender"/"receiver" vocabulary on top of which concrete executors are built. Module 6 covers this in detail.

A cross-cutting theme runs through several modules of the course — **safety**: in a number of places, C++26 changes behavior from "used to be silent UB" to "diagnosed at compile time". You've already seen an example in Module 1: out-of-bounds pack indexing used to be undefined behavior, but in C++26 it's ill-formed — a compile error. Module 2 is dedicated entirely to changes like this.

The course itself is structured as follows: 11 modules (0–10), from an introductory context lesson to debugging and removed features; the order follows dependencies, not the spec's numbering. Every written lesson has the same anatomy: motivation → theory → examples → exercises → mastery check. Exercises can be skipped — a skipped exercise gets the status "Skipped" (not "Done") and goes into the "review zone", from which it can be cleared at any time. A module is considered complete once its mastery check passes with a threshold of **80%** correct answers.

exercises.prompt: Which of the listed features is one of C++26's three flagships?
exercises.explanation: C++26's three flagships are Static Reflection (Module 5), Contracts (Module 4), and `std::execution` (Module 6). Modules, Concepts, and Ranges are C++20 features the learner is already expected to know from the course's baseline.

exercises.prompt: The learner clicked "Skip" on an exercise and never returned to it. What happens to the module?
options: The module will close as "Done", because skipping doesn't count as an error
In strict mode the module won't close as "Done"; in normal mode it may close, but the exercise stays in the "review zone" with the status "Skipped"
The course will automatically remove this lesson from navigation
Skipping invalidates the mastery check results for the whole module
exercises.explanation: "Skipped" is a separate status, not equal to "Done". The exercise accumulates in the "review zone"; in normal mode a module can close via its mastery check even with skipped exercises, but in strict mode it cannot.

questions.prompt: When was the C++26 standard ratified?
options: The date hasn't been set yet

questions.prompt: Which three features does the course call C++26's flagships?
Which cross-cutting theme runs through several modules of the course (Module 2 in particular)?
options: Safety — turning former UB into compile errors

questions.prompt: What does the "Skipped" status on an exercise mean?
options: The exercise has failed permanently and is no longer accessible
The exercise isn't counted as done, goes into the "review zone", but can be cleared later
The module will be permanently locked because of this

questions.prompt: What pass threshold of correct answers is required for a module's mastery check?
options: The threshold is set by the learner themselves
